nativeRange.habitat: Generally associated with slow-moving streams, marshes, and paddy-edge wetlands of southern and western India, especially the Western Ghats. Emersed (paddy/marsh) forms tend to have smaller, greener leaves; submersed forms develop the larger, softer, pink-magenta leaves prized in the hobby.

decorPreferences: Nutrient-rich aquasoil substrate with iron-rich root tabs (heavy macro + micro feeder)
High PAR (generally advised 60-80+) is the single biggest driver of vivid pink-magenta coloration
Reliable CO2 injection (generally targeted at 25-35 ppm) is essentially required to prevent melt
Soft acidic water (pH 5.5-6.5, low gH/kH) tends to intensify the pink-magenta tones
Plant stems with adequate spacing to prevent lower-leaf shading
Trim regularly to maintain a tight bushy hedge of red tops
Stable parameters — sensitive to ammonia spikes, nitrate swings, and sudden parameter changes

summary: An Indian stem plant generally considered the gold-standard 'red Rotala' of high-tech aquascaping, capable of arguably the most vivid pink-magenta coloration in the freshwater hobby under strong light and reliable CO2. Submersed leaves are larger and softer than R. rotundifolia. Demanding and unforgiving of parameter swings.
careNotes:
Rotala macrandra is widely considered the gold-standard 'red Rotala' of high-tech aquascaping. Submersed leaves tend to be noticeably larger and softer than those of R. rotundifolia, and the coloration in well-tuned tanks runs to a vivid pink-magenta rather than the pink-red of the rotundifolia complex. Native to South Asia, with documented presence in India and particularly associated with the Western Ghats region.

Care is generally rated advanced to difficult. The plant tends to do best with high PAR (commonly advised 60-80+), reliable CO2 injection (typically targeted around 25-35 ppm), a nutrient-rich aquasoil substrate, supplemental iron-rich root tabs, and balanced macro + micro dosing with extra iron. It has been reported to melt readily under low CO2, ammonia exposure, low phosphate (sources suggest ~1.5-2.0 ppm PO4 supports red coloration), or sudden parameter swings. Soft acidic water (pH around 5.5-6.5, low gH/kH) is generally advised to intensify magenta-red tones; harder, more alkaline water tends to push coloration toward green-orange and reduce leaf size.

Multiple named cultivars are commonly traded: 'Mini' and 'Mini Type 2' are compact dwarf forms used as midground; 'Variegated' has mottled pink-cream-green leaves; 'Green' is a non-red form with the same morphology; 'Pearl' is a smaller-leaved form. This entry covers the species-level type form.

Propagation is by stem cuttings — trim healthy tops and replant into substrate; lateral shoots from cut nodes form denser groupings over time. The plant may arrive in emersed or submerged form; emersed-grown stems will typically melt back and regrow submersed-form leaves after acclimation.

Cross-references: fw-plant-046 (R. rotundifolia), fw-plant-081 (R. indica), fw-plant-160 ('Colorata'), fw-plant-161 ('H'Ra').

Source disagreement notes: Modern Aquarium gives temperature 70-82°F (~21-28°C) and described native range as 'Southeast Asia' (likely a slight inaccuracy — the species is South Asian / Indian, per BucePlant and broader botanical literature); BucePlant gives pH 6.5-7.5 and adult height 4-12 inches (10-30 cm). pH range was extended on the acidic side based on the standard hobby convention that soft acidic water intensifies red coloration. Care level averaged between Modern Aquarium's 'moderate-to-advanced' and BucePlant's 'difficult' to 'advanced'.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium — Rotala Macrandra care
primary.notes: Primary source for temperature range, lighting (moderate-to-high), CO2 (strongly recommended), placement, growth rate, and propagation guidance. Source described native range as 'Southeast Asia' which appears to be slightly off — BucePlant and the broader literature place the species in South Asia (India).

additional.name: BucePlant — Rotala Macrandra product page
additional.notes: Secondary source. Confirms family Lythraceae, native range India, adult height 4-12 inches (10-30 cm), pH 6.5-7.5, light medium-to-high, CO2 required, difficulty 'Difficult'. Notes phosphate 1.5-2.0 ppm and nitrate <10 ppm for red coloration, fragile leaves, and tendency to melt from drastic parameter changes.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Rotala macrandra
additional.notes: Attempted as a third source for taxonomic confirmation but returned HTTP 404. Taxonomy (family Lythraceae, order Myrtales) was independently confirmed by BucePlant + standard botanical reference for the genus Rotala.
